title: Amniotic Fluid Embolism
chief_complaint: In active labor at 39 weeks. Suddenly developed severe shortness of breath, chest pain, and then collapsed. Became unresponsive. No pulse.

physical_exam.Cardiac: Cardiac arrest. PEA on monitor. CPR initiated.
physical_exam.Lungs: Severe pulmonary edema. Frothy pink sputum from ET tube.
physical_exam.Pelvic: Fully dilated. Fetal head at +2 station.
physical_exam.Skin: Cyanotic. Petechiae developing on chest and arms (DIC).
physical_exam.Fetal: Fetal bradycardia 60 bpm. Emergent C-section being performed at bedside (perimortem C-section).

hidden_data.abg: pH 6.85, PaCO2 85, PaO2 25, HCO3 8. Severe combined respiratory and metabolic acidosis.
hidden_data.echo: Severe RV dilation and dysfunction. LV underfilled, hyperdynamic. McConnell sign (RV free wall akinesis with apical sparing). Acute cor pulmonale.
hidden_data.coagulation: DIC: Platelets 30,000, INR 5.0, Fibrinogen <50, D-dimer >100,000. Massive consumption coagulopathy.
hidden_data.ekg: PEA arrest. After ROSC: Sinus tachycardia. Right heart strain pattern.

correct_diagnosis: Amniotic Fluid Embolism (AFE) with Cardiac Arrest, DIC, and ARDS - OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY
key_learning_points: AFE = amniotic fluid enters maternal circulation → anaphylactoid reaction (not truly embolic). Classic triad: Sudden cardiovascular collapse + Respiratory failure + DIC. Occurs during labor, delivery, or immediate postpartum. Incidence 1:40,000 deliveries. Mortality 20-60% (leading cause of maternal death in developed countries)

Pathophysiology: Amniotic fluid (fetal cells, hair, vernix, meconium) enters maternal pulmonary circulation → biphasic reaction: Phase 1: Pulmonary vasospasm → acute pulmonary hypertension → RV failure → cardiac arrest. Phase 2: Left ventricular failure → pulmonary edema + DIC from release of tissue factor

Clinical presentation: SUDDEN onset. Phase 1 (minutes): Dyspnea, chest pain, hypoxia, hypotension, cardiac arrest (usually PEA). Phase 2 (survived Phase 1): Hemorrhage (DIC), ARDS. Fetal distress (hypoxia from maternal collapse)

Management: NO specific treatment. SUPPORTIVE. 1) CPR with left uterine displacement (aortocaval compression). 2) Perimortem C-section within 5 minutes of arrest (improves maternal resuscitation by relieving aortocaval compression + saves fetus). 3) Intubate, ventilate (ARDS protocol - low tidal volume). 4) Treat RV failure: Inotropes (Dobutamine, Milrinone), Pulmonary vasodilators (Inhaled nitric oxide). 5) MTP for DIC (1:1:1 ratio, Cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100, Platelets for <50K). 6) ECMO if available and refractory

Differential: PE (chest pain + dyspnea, no DIC), Eclampsia (hypertension + seizures, no DIC), Anaphylaxis (urticaria, angioedema, no DIC), Septic shock (fever, gradual onset, no DIC), Hemorrhagic shock (gradual, no acute RV failure). AFE is diagnosis of exclusion. Postmortem: Fetal squamous cells in pulmonary vasculature (historical - not required for diagnosis)
